candidate_problems.text: How can 'credible political futurity' be operationalized and measured independently of constitutional survival itself? The conjecture is powerful but risks circularity unless it specifies observable indicators for actors' expected future interaction, discount rates, and perceived continuity of the regime before breakdown occurs.

What are the causal mechanisms by which mass publics, not just elites, sustain or destroy the repeated-game structure under stress? The conjecture names elites, institutions, and publics, but it remains unclear when publics act as enforcers of reciprocity, when they shorten horizons through polarization or panic, and how media and mobilization alter observability of defection.

What explains cases where constitutional orders survive despite apparently one-shot incentives, or collapse despite long-standing repeated interactions? These potential anomalies raise the open question of whether repeated-game expectations are the primary driver or whether other structural variables—state capacity, coercive control, ideology, external patrons, or constitutional legitimacy—sometimes dominate.
